Name: dynamic_rate_distortion_training
Title: Dynamic Rate-Distortion Optimization for Neural Network Training
Short Hypothesis: Integrating rate-distortion optimization directly into the training loop, rather than as a post-hoc compression step, can achieve better compression-performance trade-offs by dynamically adjusting the compression strategy based on the evolving task-relevant information.
Related Work: Existing approaches to model compression, such as pruning and quantization, often treat compression as a separate step after training. Recent work (e.g., Bu et al., 2021) has explored the information-theoretic underpinnings of compression but does not integrate these insights into the training process. Our proposal distinguishes itself by dynamically optimizing the rate-distortion trade-off during training, leveraging mutual information estimators to guide the allocation of bits where they most reduce task-relevant distortion.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to neural network training that integrates rate-distortion optimization directly into the training loop. By dynamically adjusting the compression strategy based on the evolving task-relevant information, our method aims to achieve superior compression-performance trade-offs. We frame the problem as a joint optimization of task loss and compression efficiency, using mutual information estimators to guide the allocation of bits where they most reduce task-relevant distortion. Our experiments demonstrate that this approach can achieve higher compression ratios with minimal loss in task performance compared to traditional post-hoc compression methods. We evaluate on benchmark datasets and tasks, providing insights into the fundamental limits of compression during training.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Compare dynamic rate-distortion training to traditional post-hoc compression methods (e.g., pruning, quantization) on image classification tasks (CIFAR-10, ImageNet). Metrics: compression ratio, task accuracy, bits-per-weight.
Experiment 2: Analyze the evolution of mutual information between layers and the task during training. Metrics: layer-wise mutual information, task accuracy.

Experiment 3: Investigate the impact of different distortion measures (e.g., MSE, KL divergence) on compression efficiency and task performance. Metrics: compression ratio, task accuracy, distortion measure.
Experiment 4: Evaluate the generalization performance of dynamically compressed models on out-of-distribution data. Metrics: task accuracy on OOD datasets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The dynamic optimization process may increase training time and computational overhead.
The effectiveness of the approach may depend on the choice of mutual information estimators, which can be challenging to implement accurately.
The proposed method may not generalize well to all types of neural network architectures or tasks.
There is a risk of overfitting the compression strategy to the training data, leading to poor generalization performance.
